The problem presents three tasks related to algorithms: analyzing the time complexity of a recursive merge sort algorithm (Task A), finding the shortest possible route for a traveler visiting several towns and returning to the starting point (Task B), and analyzing the time complexity of quicksort in the worst case (Task C).

## Step 2: Evaluate Task A
Task A involves analyzing the time complexity of a recursive merge sort algorithm. Merge sort is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that splits an input array into two halves, recursively sorts them, and then merges them. The time complexity of merge sort is known to be O(n log n) because with each recursive call, the size of the array is halved (log n levels), and at each level, the algorithm performs n operations to merge the sorted halves.

## Step 3: Evaluate Task B
Task B is related to the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP), which is an NP-hard problem. It involves finding the shortest possible tour that visits a set of cities and returns to the original city. The problem statement mentions asymmetric edges, meaning the distance from town A to town B is not necessarily the same as from town B to town A. Solving TSP exactly for a small number of cities can be done through brute force or specific algorithms like branch and bound, but proving optimality can be complex.

## Step 4: Evaluate Task C
Task C requires analyzing the worst-case time complexity of quicksort and explaining the importance of pivot choice. Quicksort is another divide-and-conquer algorithm that picks a pivot, partitions the array around it, and recursively sorts the subarrays. The worst-case scenario occurs when the pivot is the smallest or largest element, leading to O(n^2) time complexity. A good pivot choice can mitigate this, aiming for a more balanced partition.
